title: Navigating Short Term vs Long Term Rental Tax Rules Canada: A Comprehensive Guide for Accidental Landlords
metaDescription: Understand short term vs long term rental tax rules Canada with our expert guide, covering CRA regulations and Line item definitions for small landlords.
content:
As an accidental landlord, managing a rental property while holding down a full-time day job can be overwhelming, especially when it comes to navigating the complex world of short term vs long term rental tax rules Canada. The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) has specific guidelines for each type of rental, and understanding these differences is crucial to avoid costly mistakes and ensure compliance. In this article, we will delve into the details of short term vs long term rental tax rules Canada, providing you with the knowledge and tools necessary to manage your rental property with confidence.

## Introduction to Short Term Rentals
Short term rentals, typically defined as rentals with a term of less than one year, are subject to different tax rules than long term rentals. According to the CRA, short term rentals are considered business income, and as such, are reported on the T2125 Statement of Business or Professional Activities. This means that you will need to calculate your business income and expenses, including items such as Line 8520 Advertising, Line 8710 Interest, and Line 8960 Maintenance. For example, if you rent out your property on Airbnb for $2,000 per month, you would report this income on Line 162 of your T1 General tax return, and claim your expenses on the corresponding lines of the T2125.

## Introduction to Long Term Rentals
Long term rentals, on the other hand, are considered rental income, and are reported on the T776 Statement of Real Estate Rentals. This form is used to calculate your net rental income, which is then reported on Line 160 of your T1 General tax return. Long term rentals are subject to different expenses, such as Line 8730 Insurance, Line 8760 Property Taxes, and Line 8910 Utilities. For instance, if you rent out your property for $1,500 per month, you would report this income on Line 160 of your T1 General tax return, and claim your expenses on the corresponding lines of the T776.

## Tax Implications of Short Term vs Long Term Rentals
The tax implications of short term vs long term rentals can be significant. Short term rentals are subject to GST/HST, which must be charged to your tenants and remitted to the CRA. Long term rentals, on the other hand, are exempt from GST/HST. Additionally, short term rentals may be subject to provincial taxes, such as the Ontario tourism tax. For more information on CRA-compliant bookkeeping for Canadian landlords, see our article on [CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords: The Complete Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/cra-compliant-bookkeeping-canadian-landlords-complete-guide).

## Calculating Rental Income and Expenses
Calculating rental income and expenses is a critical step in navigating short term vs long term rental tax rules Canada. For short term rentals, you will need to calculate your business income and expenses, including items such as Line 8530 Commissions, Line 8550 Management Fees, and Line 8590 Travel Expenses. For long term rentals, you will need to calculate your net rental income, including items such as Line 8810 Gross Rent, Line 8830 Allowable Expenses, and Line 8850 Capital Cost Allowance. For example, if you have a long term rental property with a gross rent of $18,000 per year, and allowable expenses of $8,000 per year, your net rental income would be $10,000 per year.

## Common Mistakes Small Landlords Make
Small landlords often make mistakes when navigating short term vs long term rental tax rules Canada. Some common mistakes include:
* Missing structural depreciation: Failing to claim depreciation on your rental property can result in a significant loss of deductions.
* Misclassifying line items: Failing to properly classify your expenses can result in disallowed deductions.
* Not keeping accurate records: Failing to keep accurate records can result in missed deductions and potential audits. For more information on simplifying rental property accounting, see our article on [Simplifying Rental Property Accounting: Software vs Spreadsheets for Canadian Landlords](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/simplifying-rental-property-accounting-software-vs-spreadsheets-for-canadian-landlords).

## Key Takeaways
The key takeaways from this article are:
* Short term rentals are considered business income, while long term rentals are considered rental income.
* Short term rentals are subject to GST/HST, while long term rentals are exempt.
* Calculating rental income and expenses is a critical step in navigating short term vs long term rental tax rules Canada.
* Keeping accurate records is essential to avoiding mistakes and potential audits. For more information on converting a rental property to personal use, see our article on [Converting Rental Property to Personal Use in Canada: A Step-by-Step Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/converting-rental-property-to-personal-use-in-canada-a-step-by-step-guide).
